chapters.title: Lead Like Lives Depend On It

articles.title: PsiQuantum Strengthens Executive Leadership with New Appointments - The Quantum Insider
articles.snippet: PsiQuantum Strengthens Executive Leadership with New Appointments  The Quantum Insider

articles.title: Convey appoints Jim Hansen as Chief Product Officer and Britta Hormann as Chief of Staff to strengthen executive leadership team - Business Wire
articles.snippet: Convey appoints Jim Hansen as Chief Product Officer and Britta Hormann as Chief of Staff to strengthen executive leadership team  Business Wire

articles.title: Ciena bolsters executive team with supply chain, product appointments to scale AI infrastructure - SDxCentral
articles.snippet: Ciena bolsters executive team with supply chain, product appointments to scale AI infrastructure  SDxCentral

post1.text:
Most scaling companies hire executives to solve today's problems. The best ones hire for the company they are building two years from now.

When companies like Ciena add senior supply chain and product leaders specifically to scale AI infrastructure, they are not filling seats. They are making a bet on where the business is going and staffing ahead of that curve.

Here is what that means for leaders running $50M+ organizations:

1. Your org chart is a strategy document. Who sits in each seat tells the market, your team, and your investors what you believe the next chapter requires.

2. Leadership gaps at the top do not stay at the top. They cascade. Missed decisions, slow execution, and cultural drift all trace back to the wrong person in the wrong role.

3. The hardest part of scaling is not finding great people. It is knowing which roles matter most before the pressure forces your hand.

So here is the real question: Are you hiring for the business you have today, or the one you are committed to building?

#Leadership #Scaling #ExecutiveStrategy

post2.text:
A recent piece in SDxCentral caught our attention: Ciena just bolstered its executive team with targeted supply chain and product appointments, specifically to scale AI infrastructure.

This is not a routine hire story. It is a case study in intentional leadership design.

At Autobahn, we work with companies scaling from $50M to $500M and beyond, and the pattern is consistent. The businesses that scale cleanly are the ones where leadership structure is treated as a growth tool, not an org chart formality.

Ciena is not reacting to a problem. They are building the leadership capacity the next phase demands, before that phase arrives.

If you are running a scaling company, the question worth asking is whether your current executive team is built for where you are or where you are going.

Read the full article here: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMixwFBVV95cUxOQm05NW1RSUh0Uk1fa0hYMU9QTHVnaXJhZEpfdVVjaE9uWURQS3QtNGxCMWZhWjNwdWUyeXFwengxbDZZRFd6OF91ZTdtUmtNSGoyTkRzdklfeENYQ09ITWN6UjhWQVE0UE5sQmx3MXZJdmNjLVhSM2plUXl5aXhyOS1aS2hCb2xYQVU4UUdDakphV3NPWTdfazVQVWJtUEs2bFJWY3VzeTk5N2tVN3dFdE5TOFlqMUZxaWZvdTRfQ2w2QmVOUFF3?oc=5

post3.text:
Companies making headlines for strengthening their executive teams are demonstrating something our upcoming book addresses head-on.

Chapter 1 of Rock Your Business, titled "Lead Like Lives Depend On It" and authored by Katherine Slain, opens with a premise that stops most scaling leaders cold: the leadership approach that got you to $50M is not the one that will carry you to $500M.

Katherine's work in this chapter goes beyond leadership philosophy. It gets into the specific ways that founders and executives must evolve their roles, their decisions, and their standards as the company grows into new complexity.

The companies you see making smart executive appointments right now are not just filling roles. They are leading with intention at scale. That distinction is exactly what this chapter is built around.

Rock Your Business — available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: The executive team that scaled your company to $50M may be the single biggest obstacle standing between you and $500M. That is not a talent problem. It is a design problem. The fastest-scaling companies are not reacting to leadership gaps after they form. They are building intentional leadership structures ahead of the growth curve, and the difference in outcomes is not subtle.

chapters.title: Maximize Your Multiple to Achieve Your Exit

articles.title: How to Create an Exit Strategy for Your Business - U.S. Chamber of Commerce
articles.snippet: How to Create an Exit Strategy for Your Business  U.S. Chamber of Commerce

articles.title: Interview: Harlem Local Darius Ross, From Homelessness To Entrepreneurial Success - Harlem World Magazine
articles.snippet: Interview: Harlem Local Darius Ross, From Homelessness To Entrepreneurial Success  Harlem World Magazine

articles.title: Business Exit Strategies for Startup Founders & Entrepreneurs - J.P. Morgan
articles.snippet: Business Exit Strategies for Startup Founders & Entrepreneurs  J.P. Morgan

post1.text:
Most business owners wait too long to think about their exit. By the time they're ready to sell, they've already left millions on the table.

Here's what the data consistently shows: buyers don't pay for what your business did. They pay for what it's predictably going to do. If your revenue depends on you personally, your multiple shrinks. If your top three customers represent more than 30% of revenue, your multiple shrinks. If your management team can't run the company without you for 90 days, your multiple shrinks.

J.P. Morgan recently outlined how founders who build exit optionality early, rather than scrambling at the end, consistently command better terms and higher valuations. That tracks with what we see at the $50M to $500M level every day.

Three things that actually move your multiple: documented recurring revenue, a leadership bench that isn't just you, and clean financials with no surprises. These take years to build, not months.

So here's the question worth sitting with: if a qualified buyer called you tomorrow, would your business be worth what you think it is?

post2.text:
A recent piece from J.P. Morgan walks through exit strategy fundamentals for founders and entrepreneurs, and it's worth a read for anyone at the growth stage.

But here's what most exit planning content misses: the strategies that work for a $5M startup look almost nothing like what works for a $50M to $500M company. At that scale, your deal structure, your EBITDA adjustments, your management retention plan, and your buyer pool all change dramatically.

At Autobahn, we work with leaders who are scaling through exactly this window. The companies that maximize their multiple don't treat exit planning as a one-time event. They treat it as an operating discipline built into how they run the business every quarter.

If you're in that growth range and haven't stress-tested your valuation assumptions, this article is a solid starting point.

https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMixgFBVV95cUxPTU5NZlBZTkQwd29TWDREd3dhcVZjWHo4MS1JSVpOVU1UNHUtR29HNzdzR1BjS0NFbGl5V1l2UFhPMllFc1JjLVpyZEVzalhaS1VWZDdvQ1kwZGFkdDBfczlmb3o5bjU3NzR6SnhFOWdfM1ZYUkZuVEo4Qk9Ta3B6TnNJYUJ0OEVsMmZFWnVVSDlzMGZueWhGWXlSblQzYW1FOXFndkJ1Y00wODdLa296RlJ6UVhwVzQteHBpbnZCeG53Y2tSSmc

post3.text:
Exit planning is one of the most misunderstood disciplines in business. Most leaders know they should have a strategy. Very few know how to engineer a valuation that holds up under buyer scrutiny.

Chapter 2 of our upcoming book, Rock Your Business, is titled "Maximize Your Multiple to Achieve Your Exit." Authors Paul Belair and George Hohman lay out a framework for how scaling companies can systematically increase what a buyer will actually pay, not just what an owner hopes to receive.

One thing they make clear early on: your multiple is not a number you discover at closing. It's a number you build over time, through deliberate decisions about revenue quality, operational independence, and financial transparency.

If you're running a company in the $50M to $500M range and an exit is anywhere on your horizon, this chapter will change how you think about growth itself.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Most business owners treat their exit like a finish line. The ones who get the best deals treat it like a training plan. If you're running a company between $50M and $500M, the decisions you make today about revenue mix, leadership depth, and financial hygiene are already determining what a buyer will pay you. Here's what actually moves the multiple.
posts.email_subject: Your multiple is set before you list

chapters.title: What We Learned Selling Fish Food and Nike Shoes

articles.title: Wrike Now Offers a Strategic Portfolio Management Solution to Bridge Strategy and Execution, Enabling Organizations to Plan and Deliver True Transformation - Business Wire
articles.snippet: Wrike Now Offers a Strategic Portfolio Management Solution to Bridge Strategy and Execution, Enabling Organizations to Plan and Deliver True Transformation  Business Wire

articles.title: Uniserve Advances National Digital Infrastructure Strategy with $1.8 Million Committed Growth Credit Facility - TMX Newsfile
articles.snippet: Uniserve Advances National Digital Infrastructure Strategy with $1.8 Million Committed Growth Credit Facility  TMX Newsfile

articles.title: From paper to performance: How to execute your 2026 credit union strategic plan - CUInsight
articles.snippet: From paper to performance: How to execute your 2026 credit union strategic plan  CUInsight

post1.text:
Most strategic plans fail not because they were poorly written, but because execution was never actually designed.

Leaders at growing companies spend weeks building a plan, then hand it off and assume momentum will carry it forward. It won't.

A recent analysis of how organizations are bridging strategy and execution found that the gap between planning and delivery is where transformation goes to die. The companies closing that gap are doing something different: they are treating execution as a discipline, not an afterthought.

Here is what that looks like in practice for a $50M+ company:

1. Every strategic priority has a named owner, not a team or a department.
2. Progress is reviewed on a cadence that matches the pace of your market, not your fiscal calendar.
3. The plan is a living document, updated when conditions change, not defended because it took four months to build.

Strategy without an execution architecture is just expensive documentation.

How often does your leadership team actually revisit the plan versus just reporting against it?

post2.text:
A recent piece in CUInsight tackled a challenge that goes far beyond credit unions: how do you turn a strategic plan into actual performance instead of letting it collect dust after Q1?

For companies scaling past $50M, this is one of the most expensive problems they never budget for. The plan gets built, the leadership team feels aligned, and then the day-to-day takes over.

At Autobahn, we see this constantly. The organizations that scale well are not the ones with the most sophisticated plans. They are the ones with the shortest distance between decision and action.

If your 2025 plan is already drifting, that is a system problem, not a discipline problem. Fix the system.

Read the full article here and think about how it applies to your business: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMiqAFBVV95cUxOWVRBZ0VjRlV6U3JLUnBwLTdLeWktRDczdDFWaUMzRHdid0ZCQmhtdWY2dERjZi1UNVg5S0R0VXlua3N0MDJSYUNzeEJiMC1ibXhTa3dpVlNaRG54YUVCRzhYWVUydXNGLXI3VHljVEFWb3VNVkNvUHpRWTE4a0JSRU0tcEtrRGNfRHVyRDVxTWNmRnBLb1dVMmVZXzUzODI3SFNGX09jR2U

post3.text:
Bridging strategy and execution is not a technology problem. It is a leadership and accountability problem.

In Chapter 3 of Rock Your Business, titled "What We Learned Selling Fish Food and Nike Shoes," authors Rafa Loyo and Jim Randall dig into exactly this tension. Drawing from two very different industries and two very different growth journeys, they reveal something most leadership teams discover too late: the way you build a plan matters far less than whether the people executing it actually understand why it was built.

There is a specific insight in that chapter about how the best-scaling companies align their frontline operators to strategic priorities without drowning them in frameworks. It changes how you think about the distance between your boardroom and your operations floor.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Most companies treat strategic planning as an annual event and execution as someone else's job. By the time leadership notices the gap, a full quarter is already gone. The organizations scaling from $50M to $500M and beyond are not working from better plans. They are working from better systems that keep strategy alive long after the offsite ends.
posts.email_subject: Your strategy is already stalling. Here's why.

chapters.title: Every Day Without a Real Sales Strategy is a Donation

articles.title: AI Time Savings Won’t Drive Revenue Unless CSOs Intervene - Gartner
articles.snippet: AI Time Savings Won’t Drive Revenue Unless CSOs Intervene  Gartner

articles.title: B2B Sales Strategy Now Demands AI, Personalization to Survive - abasto.com
articles.snippet: B2B Sales Strategy Now Demands AI, Personalization to Survive  abasto.com

articles.title: How to Double B2B Sales Growth - Bain & Company
articles.snippet: How to Double B2B Sales Growth  Bain & Company

post1.text:
Most companies above $50M do not have a sales strategy. They have a sales habit.

There is a difference. A habit is what your team has always done. A strategy is a deliberate, documented plan that connects your revenue targets to specific customer segments, motions, and decision criteria.

Bain & Company recently published research on doubling B2B sales growth. The finding that stood out: high-performing companies treat sales architecture as a leadership decision, not a sales department decision. Growth at scale requires the executive team to own the revenue model, not just monitor it.

Three things scaling companies get wrong:

1. They mistake activity for strategy. Pipeline volume is not a plan.
2. They let top performers define the process by default. That is not repeatable.
3. They have no explicit answer to the question: who are we NOT selling to?

If you removed your top two salespeople tomorrow, would your revenue strategy survive?

That answer tells you everything.

post2.text:
A recent piece in Gartner caught our attention: AI time savings in sales will not drive revenue unless sales leaders actively redirect that recovered time toward high-value activities.

This matters enormously for companies in the $50M to $500M range.

Why? Because at that stage, your sales team is already stretched thin covering too many accounts with too little structure. AI giving them back two hours a day sounds like a win. But without a clear strategy dictating where those hours go, you are not accelerating growth. You are just creating more undirected activity.

The lever is leadership, not technology. If your chief sales officer is not defining what reps do with recovered capacity, the efficiency gain disappears into the noise.

Read the full Gartner piece here and then ask whether your sales leadership is ready to capture the return: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMiYEFVX3lxTE83YmJZYVNxUUtqV1lJanRha3hGWnFtdjJYSm5FWURzTUFrVm1uTDB2R1VRWklWVlM4cVhURDNTMVFxVmw0Ql9YZUZrdndhcC1JWFRZczVJRmxlR1REQmVvMg?oc=5

post3.text:
Recent research from Bain and Gartner keeps landing on the same uncomfortable truth: most B2B companies are leaving serious revenue on the table not because of poor execution, but because of a missing strategy.

Deb and Brandon tackle this head-on in Chapter 4 of our upcoming book, Rock Your Business: Navigating the Road from $50 Million to $500 Million and Beyond. The chapter is called "Every Day Without a Real Sales Strategy is a Donation."

The title alone should make any growth-stage CEO pause. Deb and Brandon lay out why the absence of a defined sales strategy is not neutral. It costs you money every single day. They share a specific framework for building one that scales with your organization, not just your next quarter.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Most companies above $50M have never written down their sales strategy. They have revenue targets, CRM dashboards, and a top performer they are terrified to lose. That is not a strategy. That is a liability. Here is what separates scaling companies from stalling ones, and why the gap shows up in your pipeline before it ever shows up in your financials.
posts.email_subject: Your sales strategy is costing you daily

chapters.title: What Does Your Marketing Smell Like?

articles.title: Big Pharma’s Big Brand: Inside Eli Lilly’s marketing culture - Fierce Pharma
articles.snippet: Big Pharma’s Big Brand: Inside Eli Lilly’s marketing culture  Fierce Pharma

articles.title: Why JBL is building marketing around culture not campaigns - Brand Innovators
articles.snippet: Why JBL is building marketing around culture not campaigns  Brand Innovators

articles.title: Pride in PR: Gabriel Gomez - PRWeek
articles.snippet: Pride in PR: Gabriel Gomez  PRWeek

post1.text:
Your brand is not your logo. It is not your tagline. It is the feeling people get when they interact with your company, and most $50M+ leaders have no idea what that feeling actually is.

JBL recently made a deliberate shift away from campaign-driven marketing toward building around culture. The result is a brand that compounds over time instead of resetting with every product launch. That is not a consumer brand trick. That is a business strategy.

Here is what scaling companies miss:

1. Brand consistency at $50M feels optional. At $200M, inconsistency becomes expensive. Customers, employees, and partners all make decisions based on signals you are not managing.

2. Your internal culture IS your external brand. If your team cannot describe what makes you different, your customers cannot either.

3. Marketing that chases campaigns trains your audience to wait for the next offer. Marketing built on identity creates loyalty that survives price competition.

Brand is not a department. It is an operating system.

What does your company actually stand for, and could your newest hire articulate it on day one?

post2.text:
A recent piece in Brand Innovators digs into why JBL is deliberately moving away from campaign-based marketing and building its entire approach around cultural identity instead.

For consumer brands, this is interesting. For companies scaling from $50M to $500M, this is a warning.

Most growing businesses build marketing infrastructure around campaigns because campaigns are measurable and finite. But campaigns stop the moment the budget does. Culture-driven brand equity does not.

At Autobahn, we see this gap constantly. Companies hit a growth ceiling not because their product is weak, but because their brand has no coherent identity underneath the promotions.

If your marketing requires constant fuel to stay alive, you are renting attention, not building an asset.

Read the full piece and ask yourself whether your marketing is a series of campaigns or a compounding identity: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMikgFBVV95cUxPbXpYamE0dkF6MWVkcEkwUHlnSHk5bUtIbmV4ZTlDNEk3MEpURWZDdUo5b1Q4aTMtZkIzUy1scXU3VEJUREY2Vks0NjFGV0s2QXYtejBPMm93NlRrSC00MHRSdlpQcnJqeW00akp4eFRLeTZwRGxOV1BEQmR0eEpoVXB0cFpPaUhVeTBtU3E3WGxfZw

post3.text:
JBL's move to build marketing around culture rather than campaigns is exactly the kind of shift Chapter 5 of Rock Your Business was written to address.

The chapter is called "What Does Your Marketing Smell Like?" and it was authored by Kayli Simpson and Yasmin West. The title is intentional. Smell is the sense most tied to memory and emotion, and that is precisely the point.

Kayli and Yasmin challenge scaling companies to stop asking whether their marketing is generating clicks and start asking whether it is leaving a lasting impression. There is a specific framework in Chapter 5 that helps leadership teams audit what their brand actually communicates versus what they believe it communicates. The gap between those two things is usually where growth stalls.

Most companies at $50M have a marketing function. Far fewer have a marketing identity.

Rock Your Business, by Jonathan Slain and Katherine Slain, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Most companies at $50M have a marketing budget. Very few have a brand. There is a difference, and it becomes painfully obvious at $150M when competitors with inferior products are outselling you on identity alone. The companies that scale fastest are not the ones running the most campaigns. They are the ones that know exactly what feeling they leave behind.
posts.email_subject: Does your brand have a smell?

chapters.title: The Talent Engine
chapters.authors: Caleb Hawkins, Darla Klein & Yasmin West

articles.title: OpenArt Announces Sultan Khan as Head of Talent Acquisition and HR - hrtoday.in
articles.snippet: OpenArt Announces Sultan Khan as Head of Talent Acquisition and HR  hrtoday.in

articles.title: Precision Over Scale: The New Rules of Hiring in 2026 - SHRM
articles.snippet: Precision Over Scale: The New Rules of Hiring in 2026  SHRM

articles.title: WorkWave Names Melinda Vedder as Chief Human Resources Officer - Pest Control Technology
articles.snippet: WorkWave Names Melinda Vedder as Chief Human Resources Officer  Pest Control Technology

post1.text:
Hiring more people is not a growth strategy. It is a cost problem disguised as ambition.

SHRM recently reported that leading companies in 2026 are moving away from volume hiring and toward precision hiring. That shift matters enormously for companies scaling past $50M, because the mistakes compound fast at that stage.

Here is what that means in practice:

1. Headcount targets are a vanity metric. The right question is not how many people you are adding, but whether each hire directly accelerates a specific business outcome you can measure.

2. Your hiring process reveals your culture. If your process is slow, disorganized, or inconsistent, top candidates notice before they ever accept an offer.

3. The biggest talent risk at $50M to $500M is not losing people to competitors. It is promoting the wrong people into leadership roles because the company grew faster than its talent infrastructure.

Growth does not fix a broken talent engine. It exposes one.

What is the one hire your company absolutely cannot afford to get wrong right now?

post2.text:
A recent piece in SHRM makes a compelling case that precision has replaced scale as the defining principle of smart hiring in 2026. Companies are doing less shotgun recruiting and more surgical targeting of the exact skills and leadership traits that move the needle.

For companies scaling from $50M toward $500M, this is not a trend to watch. It is a decision to make now.

At Autobahn Consultants, we see this constantly: growing companies that still hire like startups, moving fast and fixing the mismatch later. That approach gets more expensive with every growth stage. Precision hiring requires knowing your org structure 18 months ahead, not just today.

If your talent acquisition process has not been redesigned since you crossed $50M in revenue, it is time to look at it differently.

Read the full SHRM piece here: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMioAFBVV95cUxPRFVYR0VscXJpMmVqV2dwazJDTkstVDJTNG9uTlZlWHJPVkFqQ0MzZTRQSUYwUDZweWw2dE52THZKSnhBTzQtb1g5UUYzbmJTbjFJcXlVYno2ZzU4aE0yNVRwN2RIN05xTWJVWnZQejVyY2xRNmJ2SF83QmVHel9GYmdWTEdEMWZLMVk5OThMNTF0TmdKR1F4bG84cnZRZ0FU?oc=5

post3.text:
The shift toward precision hiring that SHRM is tracking is something our contributors have been building frameworks around for years.

In Chapter 6 of Rock Your Business, titled The Talent Engine, authors Caleb Hawkins, Darla Klein, and Yasmin West lay out what it actually takes to build a talent infrastructure that scales with your business rather than breaking under the weight of it.

One thing Caleb, Darla, and Yasmin are clear about: talent strategy at $50M cannot look the same as talent strategy at $200M. Most companies learn that the hard way. The chapter gives leaders a concrete way to get ahead of that gap before the cracks become crises.

The insight is straightforward, but how you apply it changes everything about how you hire, develop, and retain the people your growth depends on.

Rock Your Business — available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Most companies scaling past $50M treat hiring as a volume problem. Fill the seats, hit the headcount, keep moving. But the companies that successfully reach $500M do something different: they build a talent engine that runs ahead of growth, not behind it. Here is what separates the two approaches, and what leaders need to rethink before the next stage of scale.
posts.email_subject: Your hiring process is a growth liability

chapters.title: Death by KPIs
chapters.authors: Héctor Arias, Matt Radicelli & Jim Randall

articles.title: 14 Tools to Track KPIs for Your Business - Business.com
articles.snippet: 14 Tools to Track KPIs for Your Business  Business.com

articles.title: 30 Financial Metrics and KPIs to Measure Success in 2026 - Oracle NetSuite
articles.snippet: 30 Financial Metrics and KPIs to Measure Success in 2026  Oracle NetSuite

articles.title: The KPIs to Help Manufacturers Improve Company Performance - CLA (CliftonLarsonAllen)
articles.snippet: The KPIs to Help Manufacturers Improve Company Performance  CLA (CliftonLarsonAllen)

post1.text:
More KPIs do not make better leaders. They make paralyzed ones.

Most executives scaling past $50M fall into the same trap: they add metrics to feel in control, not to drive decisions. The dashboard grows. Clarity shrinks.

A recent analysis from Oracle NetSuite cataloging 30 financial metrics worth tracking reinforces a harder truth hiding underneath the list: most companies track far more than they act on. Volume becomes a substitute for discipline.

Here is what actually matters at scale:

First, every metric on your dashboard should have an owner, a target, and a review cadence. If it has none of those three, cut it.

Second, lagging indicators tell you what happened. If your team is only watching those, you are driving by looking in the rearview mirror.

Third, the best-run companies in the $50M to $500M range typically operate on fewer than ten metrics that genuinely predict future performance.

Fewer numbers, sharper focus, faster decisions.

How many metrics on your current dashboard have actually changed a decision in the last 90 days?

post2.text:
A recent piece in CLA (CliftonLarsonAllen) lays out the KPIs manufacturers should use to improve company performance. It is a solid framework, and it raises something we see constantly with scaling businesses: having the right metrics for your industry is only half the problem.

The other half is organizational. Who owns each number? Who has the authority to act on it? How quickly does bad data trigger a real response?

For companies moving through the $50M to $500M range, measurement infrastructure without decision infrastructure is just expensive reporting. The metrics are not the destination. The behavior they drive is.

If your team tracks performance well but still struggles to close execution gaps, that is worth a serious conversation.

Read the full article here: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMiugFBVV95cUxNc3hGVVVxVnMwVEpLcmxaNWJzaURJbERjczZaSDhlcnhvOFlHX1VZU0tETkctQjFqRVhZVkNtcFNoUWtfSmRhSmU5N3pYc25IVzlfTEs5TjFZWk1RTmhZd2JzSkpPSXVmQndRRHRBM29CaDBoWnVWRXcxMnBhWkcwT3dmWTAwSDFSeS1hYzFtRUNtd05nWE9PX25nbzRfc2ZzSjVFVURMY0hMRkdVMmtOdjVOYUluUXJIcEE

post3.text:
There is a chapter in our upcoming book that every leadership team drowning in spreadsheets needs to read.

Chapter 7 of Rock Your Business is called Death by KPIs. It was written by Hector Arias, Matt Radicelli, and Jim Randall, and it tackles one of the most common scaling killers we see: companies that measure everything and manage nothing.

The chapter does not argue against metrics. It argues for the right metrics, owned the right way, reviewed at the right frequency. One insight that will reframe how you think about your dashboard: the number of KPIs your team tracks often rises in inverse proportion to how confident leadership actually feels about the business.

More data, more doubt. That pattern is fixable. But it requires honesty about what your metrics are actually doing for your decisions.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Most leadership teams believe they have a metrics problem. They do not. They have a decision problem wearing a metrics disguise. When dashboards grow faster than accountability structures, KPIs stop driving performance and start providing cover. Here is what companies scaling from $50M to $500M consistently get wrong about measurement, and what the best operators do instead.
posts.email_subject: Are your KPIs actually killing performance?

articles.title: Performance Management Needs New Metrics in the AI Era - Harvard Business Review
articles.snippet: Performance Management Needs New Metrics in the AI Era  Harvard Business Review

articles.title: ‘Talk to My A.I. Twin’: Busy Executives Have a New Productivity Hack - The New York Times
articles.snippet: ‘Talk to My A.I. Twin’: Busy Executives Have a New Productivity Hack  The New York Times

articles.title: Artificial Intelligence, Productivity, and the Workforce: Evidence from Corporate Executives - Atlanta - Federal Reserve Bank
articles.snippet: Artificial Intelligence, Productivity, and the Workforce: Evidence from Corporate Executives  Atlanta - Federal Reserve Bank

post1.text:
Most executives are using AI to save time. The ones winning are using it to make better decisions faster.

There is a difference, and it is costing companies millions to figure that out the hard way.

Recent Federal Reserve research on AI adoption among corporate executives confirmed something we see constantly with scaling companies: productivity gains from AI are real, but they are unevenly distributed. The leaders capturing the most value are not just deploying tools. They are rethinking how work gets measured, delegated, and reviewed.

If you are running a $50M to $500M business, three things are worth your attention right now:

1. Your performance metrics were built for a pre-AI workforce. They are already outdated.
2. AI amplifies your best people and exposes your weakest processes simultaneously.
3. The bottleneck is rarely the technology. It is almost always the decision-making structure around it.

AI does not fix a broken org chart. It just makes the cracks more visible, faster.

What is the one process in your business that AI would expose as broken if you deployed it tomorrow?

post2.text:
A recent piece in The New York Times highlighted a growing trend: busy executives are deploying AI-powered digital twins to handle meetings, respond to messages, and represent their thinking without them being in the room.

It reads like science fiction. It is not.

For companies scaling past $50M, this matters beyond the novelty factor. Leadership bandwidth is one of the most under-discussed growth constraints we see. When a founder or senior executive becomes the bottleneck, growth stalls. AI tools that extend executive capacity are not a productivity hack. They are a structural solution to a real scaling problem.

The question is not whether to use them. The question is whether your team and your systems are ready to operate well when leadership is less present in every conversation.

Read the full article here: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMifkFVX3lxTE1fZnBYemx0U1pIYUJQSk1WSm1sdThzLWNxcnJmaUNqMDFSajVISUtSXzJBTWFtSW9PY0JjcUVZSjRSaTZwblhuYWg3NUM1Xy1MVmxpSWtHSUJzRmlGZjA1VHBHczEwbkl5UmswVW1qVi00Q0Z3NUtZeHZDb0lEQQ

post3.text:
Harvard Business Review recently made the case that AI is forcing companies to rethink how they measure performance entirely. We could not agree more, and this is exactly the territory Hudson Shank and Jonathan Slain tackle in Chapter 8 of our upcoming book.

The chapter is called The Third Monitor Theory, and it challenges how leaders in scaling businesses physically and mentally organize their attention in an AI-driven environment.

The insight that will stop most executives cold: the problem is not that your team lacks AI tools. The problem is that leadership has not built the right context for knowing what to do with the output those tools produce.

Adding a third monitor does not make you more strategic. Building the system around it does.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: AI is not your scaling problem. Your decision-making infrastructure is. Federal Reserve data confirms executives are seeing real productivity gains from AI, but the companies pulling ahead are not the ones with the most tools. They are the ones who rebuilt how decisions get made, measured, and delegated around those tools. Here is what that actually looks like at the $50M to $500M level.
posts.email_subject: Your AI stack won't fix this

chapters.title: How Could You Be Successful and Not Have It?
chapters.authors: Samantha Frost, Caleb Hawkins & Rafa Loyo

articles.title: Six Ways to Overcome Impostor Syndrome in a New Leadership Role - Seattle University
articles.snippet: Six Ways to Overcome Impostor Syndrome in a New Leadership Role  Seattle University

articles.title: Why impostor syndrome follows high achievers all the way to the top - Forbes Australia
articles.snippet: Why impostor syndrome follows high achievers all the way to the top  Forbes Australia

articles.title: Don’t call it imposter syndrome - Fast Company
articles.snippet: Don’t call it imposter syndrome  Fast Company

post1.text:
The most dangerous leader in a scaling company is not the one who lacks confidence. It is the one who mistakes confidence for competence.

Impostor syndrome gets framed as a weakness to fix. It is not. Research consistently shows that high achievers are disproportionately affected by it, precisely because they understand the gap between where they are and what is actually required. That awareness is a feature, not a bug.

For leaders running companies in the $50M to $500M range, here is what actually matters:

First, the feeling does not go away when you grow. It scales with the business. Leaders who wait to feel ready before making hard calls will wait forever.

Second, the executives who perform best at this stage are not the ones free of self-doubt. They are the ones who act despite it, using that tension as a signal to ask better questions and build stronger teams.

Third, suppressing the doubt does not protect your culture. Naming it does.

So here is the question worth sitting with: Is your self-doubt making you a sharper leader, or is it quietly stalling the decisions your company needs you to make?

#Leadership #ScalingBusiness #ExecutiveDevelopment

post2.text:
A recent piece in Forbes Australia makes a point that deserves more attention in boardrooms: impostor syndrome does not fade as leaders climb higher. For many high achievers, it intensifies.

At Autobahn, we see this play out constantly with companies scaling past $50M. The complexity of the business outpaces the playbooks that got them there. Leaders who were supremely confident at $20M suddenly feel exposed at $100M. That is not a character flaw. That is a growth signal.

The problem is when that signal triggers avoidance instead of action. Delayed hiring decisions. Missed strategic pivots. Over-reliance on what worked before.

If you lead a scaling company and this resonates, read the full article. Then ask yourself what decision you have been deferring because you are waiting to feel more certain.

https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMiwAFBVV95cUxPQkd2QVlTbWR6eUREcW93NXVvQm1FMXVjTWd0MFVvNGU4WGNUeFozUnhBNmhDdjlhMENwM1ZucE1xV3RGdVZHaWhkWlNaRHVMQVhwV2dKNHg2dVdTOHM3RFg0TDBJdHh6WEc2dWpNdklMVGdIMnNDVW9mWlMtdk9NZDduLWtFZWFDWVUtWmUxVE40bkFwZ2w0RV9JdERmc05sV0VWT0k2WmhlR0o2ZEo0RlR2U0FBUnpHZWpGYUJOU3Y

post3.text:
Chapter 9 of Rock Your Business is titled "How Could You Be Successful and Not Have It?" and it tackles something most leadership books skip entirely: the psychological weight that comes with scaling a company past $50M.

Authored by Samantha Frost, Caleb Hawkins, and Rafa Loyo, the chapter confronts impostor syndrome directly, not as a personal failing, but as a predictable pattern that emerges at specific stages of business growth.

One insight the chapter surfaces early: the leaders most likely to struggle with impostor syndrome are often the ones the company most needs to step up. The doubt and the capability tend to arrive together.

What the chapter gives you is a way to work with that tension rather than hide from it or be paralyzed by it.

Rock Your Business, the second book from Jonathan Slain and Katherine Slain, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Every leader at the $50M to $500M stage hits a moment where the company has grown faster than their confidence. That gap has a name, and pretending it does not exist is costing scaling companies more than they realize. Here is what the research shows, what experienced executives actually do about it, and why the leaders who name it out loud tend to outperform the ones who do not.
posts.email_subject: Your doubt is not the problem. This is.

articles.title: Morgan Stanley on life after selling your business: a roadmap for entrepreneurs - Fortune
articles.snippet: Morgan Stanley on life after selling your business: a roadmap for entrepreneurs  Fortune

articles.title: The data says founders would do it again. Even in this economy. - Shopify
articles.snippet: The data says founders would do it again. Even in this economy.  Shopify

post1.text:
Most founders treat life planning as something they'll get to after the business is built. That's exactly backwards.

Morgan Stanley recently published research on entrepreneurs navigating life after an exit, and the pattern is consistent: leaders who never defined what they were building toward end up disoriented the moment they achieve the thing they spent a decade chasing.

For executives scaling through the $50M to $500M range, here's what actually matters:

1. Your business will demand everything you let it take. You have to define the boundary before it's tested, not after.

2. Life planning is not a retirement exercise. It's a performance tool. Clarity about your personal priorities makes you a faster, more decisive leader.

3. The founders who scale well have usually mapped what they want their life to look like at each stage of growth, not just what they want the business to look like.

Building a $500M company is hard. Building one while losing the life you intended to fund is harder.

What would you do differently if you planned your life with the same rigor you plan your business?

post2.text:
A recent piece in Fortune, drawing on Morgan Stanley research, laid out what entrepreneurs actually face after selling a business. The short version: most are not prepared for it.

That's a problem that starts long before the exit.

At Autobahn, we see it consistently with scaling companies. Leaders are so locked into hitting the next revenue milestone that they never ask a more important question: what is this all actually for?

Companies that scale cleanly from $50M to $500M tend to have founders and executives who have thought seriously about their own life design, not just their org chart. The two are connected more than most want to admit.

If your growth plan does not include a personal plan, you are missing a variable that will eventually matter.

Read the full Fortune piece here: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMiiwFBVV95cUxPdnZpWG94aDF5el9jOUtxNWhVYlFXZ3FKejZHQkJEOTVVSmN2VE1COEtTb3lkTnJ1VENGTVoxWGUzOHhYdlJ4QUV6bW9IajBVTEQ2cm5vUWRST3pCcWlxXzNzNHNMQlRmSURudzZZbFNuWkx2YUZWakN5ME1mcVU0Rm9XcFdMOXQzNFo4?oc=5

post3.text:
Fortune recently highlighted what happens to entrepreneurs after they sell: many find themselves without a roadmap for what comes next. It turns out building toward something undefined is its own kind of trap.

Chapter 10 of Rock Your Business, titled Coming Up for Air, takes this head-on. Written by Samantha Frost alongside Jonathan and Katherine Slain, the chapter addresses something most business books skip entirely: how to design your life alongside your company, not in spite of it.

One thing the chapter makes clear is that the leaders who scale with the least regret are not the ones who sacrificed everything. They are the ones who got specific early about what they were actually building toward.

That distinction is more actionable than it sounds, and the chapter shows you how to work with it.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Most scaling executives have a five-year plan for their business and no plan at all for their life. That gap does not stay quiet forever. Research on post-exit entrepreneurs makes it plain: the absence of intentional life design is not a personal problem you deal with later. It is a leadership problem you are already carrying. Here is what high-growth leaders are doing differently.
posts.email_subject: Your business plan is missing something
